methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series C → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.7x) to $180M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 3 same-sector + same-stage peers in Climate & Energy. Range: $700M - $2.0B.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

private.name: KoBold Metals
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series C), post-money $2B+

private.name: Electric Hydrogen
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series C), post-money $1B+

private.name: Amogy
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series C), post-money $700M

data.narrative:
Zanskar, a Series C climate and energy company founded by Carl Hoiland and Joel Edwards, has raised $180M to date. The valuation analysis draws on a peer set of three same-sector, same-stage private companies (KoBold Metals at $2.0B, Electric Hydrogen at $1.0B, and Amogy at $700M), alongside one public comparable, and employs two triangulation methods.

The stage-progression method applies a 1.7x step-up multiplier to the $180M raised proxy, implying a $306M valuation. The peer-median approach anchors to the $1.0B median across the three private comparables, with a range spanning $700M to $2.0B. These methods yield a triangulated valuation range of $260M (low) to $1.1B (high), with a median of $653M. The gap between the stage-progression result and peer median reflects typical variance in capital efficiency and market perception within early-stage climate tech, where fundraising multiples and peer valuations can diverge significantly.

Key uncertainties include the representativeness of the three-company peer set, which remains small for a $180M+ Series C, and the extent to which Zanskar's technology maturity and market position align with the median peer. The wide dispersion in peer valuations ($700M to $2.0B) underscores material uncertainty around capital intensity, path to commercialization, and investor conviction in the subsector. Confidence in any point estimate within the range should remain modest without additional operational or market-specific data.
